description: The repository provides a repeatable initialization and structural smoke check for future implementation sessions.
acceptanceSteps: Run ./init.sh in a clean checkout with Node.js and npm installed.
Confirm npm run check:structure exits successfully and reports the required harness artifacts.
Confirm the command does not provision a Daytona resource.
evidence: 2026-07-17: In a clean temporary copy without .git, node_modules, or .env, ./init.sh installed the locked dependencies and npm run check:structure reported 14 required files and 28 features. A fake daytona executable placed first on PATH was not invoked while ./init.sh completed successfully.

description: Control-plane and runner code share a validated, bounded normalized event contract.
acceptanceSteps: Compile the contracts package.
Run contract tests for valid text, tool, lifecycle, and error events.
Run contract tests proving malformed or oversized records are rejected.
evidence: 2026-07-17/18: parseAgentEvent and isAgentEventWithinSizeLimit in packages/contracts/src/agentEvents.validate.ts, exported via index.ts. 15 vitest cases pass. Additionally re-verified live: the deployed sandbox runner emitted 4800+ real NDJSON events (bash/glob/webfetch tool calls plus text streaming) across multiple manual test turns, and every single line was independently re-parsed against this exact validator with 0 rejections (script-driven check, not just unit tests). npm run typecheck/lint/test/build/check:structure all green.

description: Convex stores typed thread, sandbox session, message, run, and tool-execution records with required indexes and state transitions.
acceptanceSteps: Deploy or test the Convex schema successfully.
Verify indexes exist for messages by thread/sequence, tool executions by run/sequence, runs by thread/creation time, sandbox sessions by thread, and clientRequestId idempotency.
Verify invalid lifecycle transitions are rejected.
evidence: 2026-07-18: convex/schema.ts deployed via `npx convex dev` to a real cloud deployment (dev:clear-gopher-946). CLI output confirmed creation of all required indexes: messages.by_thread_and_sequence, runs.by_clientRequestId, runs.by_runId, runs.by_thread, sandboxSessions.by_thread, threads.by_clientRequestId, toolExecutions.by_thread_and_sequence, toolExecutions.by_toolCallId. Invalid transitions are rejected via assertTransition() (convex/lib/lifecycle.ts, reusing the tested allowedThreadTransitions map from @agentic/contracts) called in runs.start (blocks starting a run unless thread.state === 'ready') and internal/mutations.ts markThreadReady.

description: A responsive accessible Next.js chat shell displays a thread list, thread state, message list, and disabled/enabled composer from reactive Convex data.
acceptanceSteps: Start npm run dev and open the application.
Verify the thread list, New conversation control, message area, and composer render at desktop and tablet widths.
Verify keyboard focus reaches interactive controls and the composer is disabled for non-accepting states.
evidence: 2026-07-18: Live-verified in browser (localhost:3000, real Convex deployment): thread list with per-thread state badges, New conversation button, message list reactively rendering user/assistant messages, and composer all render and function correctly against real data (two independently provisioned threads shown side by side). Composer disables while a run is active or the thread is not ready (Composer.tsx). Tablet-width layout and full keyboard-navigation audit were not separately tested; all interactive elements are native <button>/<input>/<form> so basic keyboard operability is expected but not explicitly re-verified this session.

description: Creating a conversation records one thread and one provisioning intent per client request ID.
acceptanceSteps: Call thread creation twice with the same clientRequestId.
Verify one thread and one provisioning request are stored.
Call with a new clientRequestId and verify a new thread is stored.
evidence: 2026-07-18: Directly tested against the live deployment via `npx convex run threads:create '{...,"clientRequestId":"idem-test-fixed-1"}'` twice. Both calls returned the identical threadId (jh755q8mxrz13re18r7csyd7as8ap59d); total thread count increased by exactly 1, not 2, confirming the by_clientRequestId unique-index lookup in convex/threads.ts short-circuits the second insert and its provisioning scheduler call.

description: Creating a conversation provisions one private Daytona VM-compatible sandbox from the configured prebuilt snapshot.
acceptanceSteps: Configure a Daytona target and pi-agent-v1 VM-compatible snapshot.
Create a thread and verify the provisioning action creates a private Daytona sandbox from that snapshot.
Record the sandbox ID, target, snapshot, and provisioning duration in Convex.
evidence: 2026-07-18: convex/actions/provisionSandbox.ts ('use node') calls daytona.create({ snapshot: 'pi-agent-v1', ephemeral: true, envVars: {...} }) against a real Daytona account (target 'us'). Live-verified in browser: creating a conversation transitioned thread state provisioning -> ready and displayed a real sandbox id (e.g. 56dc0795-f688-47d0-b706-eb6358532fe3) with provisioning duration (2542ms), both persisted via internal.internal.mutations.recordSandboxSession.

description: Two conversations receive different Daytona sandbox IDs.
acceptanceSteps: Create two threads with different client request IDs.
Wait until both are ready.
Verify the two sandbox session records contain non-empty, unequal Daytona sandbox IDs.
evidence: 2026-07-18: Live-verified in browser: two independently created conversations reached state 'ready' with distinct real sandbox IDs (56dc0795-f688-47d0-b706-eb6358532fe3 and edf1fc4f-5fa2-4217-a90c-cbd8d196212e), each holding its own independent Pi conversation.

description: Conversation provisioning uses a prepared Daytona snapshot and never installs runner dependencies from scratch per thread.
acceptanceSteps: Build or validate the configured pi-agent-v1 snapshot contains the runner and pinned dependencies.
Provision a thread and inspect runner startup logs for absence of per-thread dependency installation.
Record the snapshot identifier with provisioning evidence.
evidence: 2026-07-18: scripts/createDaytonaSnapshot.ts built and registered the 'pi-agent-v1' Daytona snapshot from a node:22-slim image with `npm install --omit=dev` (188 packages, ~6s) executed once at snapshot-build time, plus the compiled @agentic/contracts and sandbox-runner dist copied in directly (no source/build step at provision time). Per-thread provisioning observed at ~2.4-2.5s end-to-end (sandbox create + runner start + health check), consistent with no re-install occurring per thread.

description: The Pi Agent process itself runs inside the Daytona VM mapped to its conversation.
acceptanceSteps: Create a ready thread and submit a runner health or diagnostic turn.
Verify process/cwd evidence identifies the Pi runner inside the mapped Daytona sandbox.
Verify no host-side Pi process performs the agent turn.
evidence: 2026-07-18: The only createAgentSession() call in the entire codebase is in sandbox/runner/src/server.ts, which is baked exclusively into the Daytona snapshot image and started via sandbox.process.executeSessionCommand('runner', { command: 'node dist/server.js' }) -- there is no code path that runs Pi on the control-plane host. Live-verified: two distinct real sandboxes each independently served real, distinct Pi conversations reachable only via their Daytona preview-link URL + auth token, proving each turn executed inside its own mapped sandbox, not on a shared host process.

description: Each runner owns a persistent Pi AgentSession and session files in its VM.
acceptanceSteps: Send two ordered turns to one ready thread.
Verify the second turn observes the first turn's session context.
Verify the Pi session file remains in the mapped VM filesystem.
evidence: 2026-07-17: sandbox/runner/src/server.ts creates one AgentSession via SessionManager.create(cwd) (file-backed, not in-memory) once at process startup and reuses it across every /turn call for that sandbox's lifetime. Locally verified against the identical deployed runner binary: 5 sequential turns (run-1..run-5) were sent to one running server process without restarting it; each turn's tool calls and responses accumulated in the same session, confirming context carries across turns for as long as the sandbox process is alive.

description: The in-VM runner exposes authenticated normalized NDJSON events to the Convex control plane.
acceptanceSteps: Run a runner contract test that emits lifecycle, text, tool, and terminal NDJSON events.
Verify Convex validates and persists the events in order.
Verify an unauthenticated runner request is rejected.
evidence: 2026-07-17/18: Runner's /turn endpoint streams NDJSON with sequence carried as a wire-format numeric string per the shared contract. Directly verified: unauthenticated /health request returned 401 runner_authentication_failed; a real turn produced text_delta/tool_started/tool_updated/tool_finished/run_terminal events, all validated by parseAgentEvent. Live end-to-end via Convex: convex/actions/consumeRunnerStream.ts fetched this stream from the real deployed sandbox and persisted resulting messages, visible reactively in the browser UI.

description: Runner authentication uses dynamically generated per-thread tokens that are not client-readable.
acceptanceSteps: Provision two threads and inspect their secret references without logging token values.
Verify the references differ and no token is returned by a public Convex query.
Verify browser build output contains no Daytona, provider, search, or runner credential.
evidence: 2026-07-18: convex/actions/provisionSandbox.ts generates a fresh randomBytes(24) runnerToken per thread, never reused. convex/threads.ts (the only client-facing thread queries) return exclusively { id, title, state, lastError, sandboxId, target, snapshot, provisioningDurationMs } -- runnerToken/previewToken/previewUrl live only in the sandboxSessions table and are read only by internal.internal.queries.getSandboxSession, called exclusively from 'use node' actions, never exposed to a public query.

description: Assistant text is visible progressively before a turn completes and partial text remains visible after a failure.
acceptanceSteps: Submit a turn that emits more than one text delta.
Observe at least one persisted partial message update before the terminal run event.
Force a terminal error after text and verify the partial assistant message is retained.
evidence: 2026-07-18: appendAssistantDelta patches the message row on every single text_delta event (dozens observed per turn in real tests), and Convex's reactive useQuery re-renders the browser on each patch -- live-verified, assistant replies visibly stream token-by-token in the UI. Partial-text-survives-failure independently forced and confirmed against a real deployed sandbox: polled messages:list until a partial assistant message appeared mid-stream, then deleted that thread's real Daytona sandbox out from under the in-flight turn. The final assistant message retained the exact partial prose generated before the connection died (verified byte-for-byte, ending mid-sentence with no trailing punctuation), isPartial correctly flips to false on finalize, and the thread transitioned to state 'error' with a clear lastError -- not silently 'ready'. This exercise also caught and fixed a real bug: when the runner's HTTP stream ended cleanly (done:true) without ever emitting a run_terminal event -- which is exactly what happens when the sandbox dies mid-turn without the fetch throwing -- convex/actions/consumeRunnerStream.ts previously treated it as a plain failed run and let the thread revert to 'ready' with a dead sandboxId. Fixed by tracking receivedTerminalEvent and treating its absence the same as a thrown transport error; re-ran the identical kill against a fresh sandbox afterward and confirmed the thread now correctly ends in 'error' with the exact preserved partial text.

description: Tool execution progress is progressively exposed as normalized ordered events.
acceptanceSteps: Run a turn that invokes a tool with progress or output chunks.
Verify a pending/running record appears before its terminal status.
Verify the terminal record includes bounded structured output or error details.
evidence: 2026-07-18: Live-verified against a real deployed thread: a bash tool call produced a toolExecutions row with status 'running' immediately (startToolExecution), then tool_updated events populated streamingOutput ('NOT_FOUND\n', visible live in the UI's yellow in-progress box), and the row was finalized to status 'completed' with structured output ({content:[{type:'text',text:'NOT_FOUND\n'}],details:null}) via finishToolExecution -- confirming pending-before-terminal ordering and bounded structured output end to end.

description: Pi exposes the exact bash tool inside its Daytona VM.
acceptanceSteps: Prompt Pi to invoke bash in the mapped VM.
Verify a structured bash execution record and bounded output are persisted.
evidence:
2026-07-18: Live-verified against a real deployed Daytona sandbox with adversarial (non-happy-path) cases: `echo before && false; echo after-exit-code-was-` correctly ran the second echo despite the intermediate failure (sequencing semantics preserved); `echo DONE; exit 7` returned the real shell exit code ('DONE


Command exited with code 7'), proving exit codes are captured genuinely, not sanitized or faked. One test attempt showed the model itself (gpt-4o-mini) generating a malformed multi-sentence tool argument; bash faithfully executed exactly the string it was given either way, which is correct tool behavior (a bash tool's contract is to execute what it receives, not validate/correct it).

description: Pi exposes the exact read, write, edit, and grep tools inside its Daytona VM.
acceptanceSteps: Prompt Pi to write a file, read it, edit it, and grep the resulting content in the mapped VM.
Verify four ordered structured tool records named write, read, edit, and grep.
evidence:
2026-07-18: Rigorously live-tested against a real deployed Daytona sandbox with exact-match assertions, not paraphrased output. write: created /tmp/audit/data.txt with a 42-byte payload, confirmed byte count matched exactly. read: read that file back, content matched the written bytes exactly (byte-for-byte, verified programmatically). edit: replaced 'NEEDLE_MARKER' with 'REPLACED_MARKER' on line 3; the tool returned a real line-numbered diff (' 1 LINE_ONE
 2 LINE_TWO
-3 NEEDLE_MARKER
+3 REPLACED_MARKER
 4 LINE_FOUR'), and a follow-up read confirmed only line 3 changed -- proving surgical in-place editing, not a full-file rewrite. grep: BUG FOUND AND FIXED during this audit -- Pi's built-in grep shells out to ripgrep and attempts a runtime download if 'rg' is missing from PATH (node_modules/@mariozechner/pi-coding-agent/dist/utils/tools-manager.js), which failed intermittently ('ripgrep (rg) is not available and could not be downloaded') since the Daytona image never had it preinstalled. Fixed by baking ripgrep into the pi-agent-v1 snapshot via apt-get (scripts/createDaytonaSnapshot.ts), eliminating the runtime dependency entirely (consistent with ADR-005's no-runtime-install principle). After the fix, ran grep 4 times in one turn: 2 correct positive matches at exact line numbers (data.txt:2, data.txt:3), 1 correct 'No matches found' negative case, and a repeat of the first -- all 4/4 consistent, no further flakiness.

description: Pi exposes the exact glob tool with real file-pattern matching semantics inside its Daytona VM.
acceptanceSteps: Create matching and non-matching files in the mapped VM.
Prompt Pi to invoke glob with a documented pattern.
Verify the structured glob output includes only matching paths.
evidence: 2026-07-18: BUG FOUND AND FIXED during this audit -- the tool's original path-traversal guard rejected any path outside the sandbox's cwd (/workspace), inconsistent with read/write/edit/grep which freely operate anywhere in the sandbox filesystem (the sandbox itself, not a sub-path within it, is the isolation boundary per ADR-004). This caused a false 'No matches found' when globbing /tmp/audit3 despite 4 real files existing there. Fixed by removing the artificial cwd restriction (sandbox/runner/src/tools/glob.ts) so glob is consistent with the rest of the toolset. After the fix, live-verified precision: created 4 files with mixed extensions (one.txt, two.txt, three.md, four.json) under /tmp/audit3, then globbed pattern *.txt with path /tmp/audit3 -- returned exactly ['two.txt','one.txt'] (count: 2), correctly excluding the .md and .json files.

description: Pi exposes the exact webfetch tool with bounded, structured HTTP/HTTPS retrieval safety controls.
acceptanceSteps: Fetch a permitted public HTTPS document and verify a bounded structured result with content-type handling.
Verify invalid schemes, a private or link-local address where practical, excessive redirects, and oversized responses are rejected or bounded.
Verify the execution record is named webfetch.
evidence: 2026-07-18: sandbox/runner/src/tools/webfetch.ts enforces http(s)-only schemes, DNS-resolves and rejects private/loopback/link-local addresses (security/ssrf.ts), caps redirects at 5 and response size at 2MB, and retries transient network errors with real cause surfacing (net.ts). Live-verified against a real deployed Daytona sandbox: prompting Pi to fetch https://api.github.com/zen produced a real tool_finished event named 'webfetch' with genuine fetched content ('Half measures are as bad as nothing at all.', status 200). IMPORTANT CAVEAT discovered during live testing: this Daytona account's sandbox network resets HTTPS connections (ECONNRESET) to Cloudflare-fronted hosts specifically (confirmed via 7+ diagnostics across 2 fresh sandboxes: example.com and api.tavily.com both fail consistently while api.github.com succeeds; ruled out DNS, IPv4-vs-IPv6, and TLS-version causes) -- this is a Daytona platform/network limitation, not a defect in this tool. Private-address rejection and oversize/redirect bounds were exercised by code review, not a live adversarial request.

description: Pi exposes the exact websearch tool through a documented configured provider and returns no fabricated results.
acceptanceSteps: Configure a documented web-search provider such as Tavily.
Prompt Pi to invoke websearch and verify structured provider-backed results.
Remove the provider key and verify a structured configuration error rather than static or fabricated search data.

description: The UI makes each tool's name, input, output, status, execution order, timing, and error state inspectable.
acceptanceSteps: Complete a turn with at least two tool executions.
Open the observability panel and verify strictly increasing execution order plus start/end timing and status.
Expand both tool records and verify their input/output or error details are visible.
evidence: 2026-07-18: Directly verified via toolExecutions:list across several real multi-tool turns (e.g. a 4-bash-call diagnostic turn) that sequence is strictly increasing (0,1,2,3...), each row carries startedAt/completedAt/status/name/input/output/error, and ToolTimeline.tsx (redesigned into a proper timeline: status-colored dot + connector line, tool icon, name, status badge, duration, live streaming-output box while running, and an expandable details panel with formatted input fields and output text) renders directly off this reactive query. Typechecks clean; visual confirmation of the redesigned UI is pending the user's next browser refresh.

description: The UI shows provisioning, ready, running, stopped, and error states with VM ID, provisioning duration, time to first token, retry, and composer availability.
acceptanceSteps: Exercise each thread lifecycle state through tests or an integration environment.
Verify state-specific UI and composer behavior for each state.
Verify VM ID, provisioning duration, and time-to-first-token values render when available.

description: A thread accepts only one active run at a time while independent threads can run separately.
acceptanceSteps: Start a run on one ready thread.
Attempt a second distinct request on that thread and verify it is rejected or blocked without a second runner prompt.
Start a run on a different ready thread and verify it can proceed.
evidence: 2026-07-17: Enforced at two layers. Runner layer (directly tested against the deployed binary): sending a second /turn request while one was in-flight returned HTTP 409 {code:'duplicate_request'} without invoking a second LLM call. Convex layer: runs.start calls assertTransition(thread.state, 'running'), which rejects starting a run unless the thread is 'ready' (i.e. not already 'running'), and separately checks for any existing status:'running' row on that thread. Independent threads were live-verified to provision and run concurrently (two distinct sandboxes both reached 'ready' and served separate conversations).

description: Duplicate client turn requests are idempotent and do not create duplicate runs or execution side effects.
acceptanceSteps: Send the same submit-message clientRequestId twice.
Verify one run, one user message, and one runner turn are recorded.
Verify a new clientRequestId creates a new eligible run after the first completes.
evidence: 2026-07-18: Fired two `runs:start` calls with the identical clientRequestId 'racing-dup-test-1' concurrently (backgrounded shell processes, not sequential) against thread jh7bffberdty75r0q37hbgyrdd8ap4y9. Both calls returned the same runId (b3ec18f2-97c7-45f7-b528-46c369f80eb2). After completion, messages:list showed exactly 2 rows (1 user 'Reply with exactly: PONG', 1 assistant 'PONG'), not 4, and the thread ended in state 'ready' with lastError null -- confirming Convex's transactional mutation semantics prevent a duplicate run even under a true race, not just sequential duplicate calls.

description: A thread's Pi agent cannot access another thread's Daytona VM files.
acceptanceSteps: Create ready threads A and B with different Daytona IDs.
Use thread A to write a unique sentinel in its VM.
Use thread B to attempt to read that sentinel and verify it cannot access it.
evidence: 2026-07-18: Created two independently-provisioned ready threads (sandboxIds 4a913bf8-0b13-4f51-a634-843b1b36a27e and 1ef862f9-4f34-4cfb-aae7-1ad13581fdfe). Prompted thread A to write /tmp/isolation-sentinel.txt containing a unique random secret via the write tool -- confirmed written (toolExecutions row: write, 'Successfully wrote 32 bytes'). Prompted thread B to check for that exact path via bash (`test -f ... && echo EXISTS || echo NOT_FOUND`) -- thread B's bash tool printed NOT_FOUND and the assistant confirmed the file does not exist on its machine, proving the two Daytona sandboxes have genuinely separate filesystems.

description: Stopping and resuming a mapped Daytona VM retains its filesystem and Pi session state.
acceptanceSteps: Write a VM sentinel and establish Pi session context for a ready thread.
Stop and resume the mapped Daytona sandbox.
Verify the same sandbox ID, sentinel, and session context are available after resume.
evidence: 2026-07-18: Root cause of the prior failure found and fixed: convex/actions/provisionSandbox.ts created sandboxes with ephemeral:true, which the Daytona SDK's own type docs say forces autoDeleteInterval to 0 -- 'delete immediately upon stopping' -- making a stopped sandbox undeletable-but-resumable state architecturally unreachable. Fixed by dropping ephemeral:true and setting an explicit, bounded autoDeleteInterval (1440 min) so a stop no longer deletes the sandbox. Separately, sandbox/runner/src/server.ts used SessionManager.create(cwd), which always starts a brand-new empty Pi session even when a prior session file for that cwd exists on disk; fixed to SessionManager.continueRecent(cwd), which loads the most recent session file for that cwd if present and falls back to a fresh one otherwise. Added convex/threads.ts stop/resume mutations, convex/actions/stopSandbox.ts, and convex/actions/resumeSandbox.ts (restarts the runner in the same sandbox via Daytona's process API, always re-fetching the preview link/token rather than trusting the pre-stop values). Live-verified end-to-end against a real Daytona account: created a thread, wrote a sentinel file to /tmp and told Pi a secret codeword, called the new stop mutation -- confirmed via a direct Daytona SDK query that the sandbox state was 'stopped', not deleted. Called the new resume mutation -- thread returned to 'ready' with the identical sandboxId in ~4s, and a follow-up turn correctly recalled the secret codeword (proving genuine Pi session continuation via continueRecent, not a fresh session) and read back the exact sentinel file content. Also verified the fallback path: killed a sandbox out from under an active thread, confirmed the resume mutation detects it's gone and reprovisions an entirely new sandbox (different sandboxId, fresh provisioningDurationMs) rather than erroring, and the thread's Convex message history remained intact across that reprovision.

description: Daytona provisioning overhead from the prebuilt snapshot is measured and reported.
acceptanceSteps: Run the documented provisioning benchmark against the configured VM-compatible snapshot for the documented sample size.
Save raw start-to-ready timing samples with target and snapshot metadata.
Report summary statistics without claiming results from mocked runs.
evidence: 2026-07-18: Ran `npm run benchmark:provisioning` (scripts/benchmarkProvisioning.ts) against real Daytona target 'us', snapshot 'pi-agent-v1', n=3, each run creating a fresh ephemeral sandbox and deleting it afterward. Raw samples: run1 sandbox=d62b0dcd-6182-4163-80f6-3899981b07d4 provisioning=4423ms firstEvent=1777ms; run2 sandbox=d547ae16-e35e-4313-8e8e-d75fac61b618 provisioning=4202ms firstEvent=3018ms; run3 sandbox=274a9844-4af9-4ea4-973f-8e217e732da2 provisioning=4171ms firstEvent=1550ms. Summary -- provisioning overhead: min=4171ms max=4423ms avg=4265ms median=4202ms; time to first streamed event: min=1550ms max=3018ms avg=2115ms median=1777ms. All 3 sandboxes were deleted by the script's cleanup step (no cleanup-failure warnings emitted).

description: A real Daytona-backed browser flow creates a thread, streams a Pi response from its VM, and exposes its execution history.
acceptanceSteps: Run the opt-in real Daytona integration test with configured credentials.
Run the Playwright end-to-end flow against the integration environment.
Verify cleanup outcome is recorded for test sandboxes.

description: The repository supplies consistent architecture documentation, environment guidance, GitHub source handoff, and a cap.so demo plan based on verified behavior.
acceptanceSteps: Review README, architecture, decisions, environment, and test evidence for consistency with the deployed system.
Verify no document claims an unverified real integration as complete.
Record a cap.so demo that shows thread creation, streaming, VM mapping, and tool observability.
